title: Private costs and benefits, externalities and social costs and benefits
paperName: Cambridge International A Level Economics (P3 & P4)
summary: 9708 A Level externalities — MPC, MSC, MPB, MSB, and welfare analysis with GeoGebra.
learningObjectives: Distinguish private and social costs and benefits.
Analyse negative and positive externalities on diagrams.

simpleExplanation.title: Spillover costs and benefits
simpleExplanation.summary: Externalities cause a wedge between private and social curves. Negative externalities lead to overproduction relative to the social optimum.
steps: Pigouvian tax = external cost per unit at optimum.

flashcards.back: MPC = private marginal cost to the producer. MSC = MPC + marginal external cost. MSC is the true cost to society.
MPB = private marginal benefit to the consumer. MSB = MPB + marginal external benefit. MSB is the true benefit to society.
MSC > MPC — e.g. pollution. Market overproduces: Q_market > Q_social where MSB = MSC.
MSB > MPB — e.g. vaccinations. Market underproduces: Q_market < Q_social.
A per-unit tax equal to the marginal external cost at the social optimum — internalises the externality and moves Q_market toward Q_social.
A per-unit subsidy equal to the marginal external benefit at the social optimum — encourages more consumption/production of goods with positive externalities.

sections.content: **Externalities** (7.4) are core A Level diagram work. Shade **DWL** and label **Q_market** vs **Q_social** on every answer. Distinguish **production** vs **consumption** externalities.
Private vs social costs and benefits

Markets equate **MPC = MPB** (private equilibrium). Society's optimum is where **MSC = MSB**.

**MSC = MPC + marginal external cost (MEC)**
**MSB = MPB + marginal external benefit (MEB)**

When external costs exist, MSC lies **above** MPC. When external benefits exist, MSB lies **above** MPB.

Social optimum: $MSB = MSC$

Negative externality: $Q_{market} > Q_{social}$

Positive externality: $Q_{market} < Q_{social}$

Example: factory pollution. Producers consider only **MPC**, not harm to neighbours. Market produces **too much** — Q_market > Q_social.

**Corrective policies:**
- **Pigouvian tax** per unit = MEC at Q_social
- **Regulation** (emission limits, permits)
- **Tradable pollution permits** (cap and trade)

Example: education or vaccinations. Individual considers only **MPB**, not benefits to society (educated workforce, herd immunity). Market produces **too little** — Q_market < Q_social.

**Corrective policies:**
- **Subsidies** per unit = MEB at Q_social
- **Direct provision** (state schools, free vaccinations)
- **Information campaigns** to raise perceived benefits

sections.question:
The market for pesticide use is shown below (linear curves):

- MPC = 10 + 0.5Q
- External cost = 4 per unit (constant)
- MPB = MSB = 30 − 0.5Q

(a) Find Q_market and Q_social.
(b) Calculate the Pigouvian tax per unit.
(c) Shade and describe the deadweight loss.
sections.solution:
(a) **Q_market:** MPC = MPB
10 + 0.5Q = 30 − 0.5Q
Q = **20 units**, P = £20

**Q_social:** MSC = MSB, where MSC = MPC + 4 = 14 + 0.5Q
14 + 0.5Q = 30 − 0.5Q
Q = **16 units**, P = £22

Market **overproduces** by 4 units (20 − 16).

(b) **Pigouvian tax** = marginal external cost = **£4 per unit** (constant in this case).

With tax, effective MPC = 10 + 0.5Q + 4 = 14 + 0.5Q = MSC → Q falls to 16.

(c) **DWL:** Triangle between MSC and MSB from Q_social (16) to Q_market (20).

At Q = 20: MSC = 14 + 10 = £24, MSB = £20 → gap = £4.
At Q = 16: MSC = MSB = £22 → gap = £0.

DWL = ½ × (20 − 16) × £4 = **£8** — welfare lost from the 4 extra units.

sections.content: Label curves **MPC, MSC, MPB, MSB** — never just S and D in externality questions. Show **Q_market** at MPC = MPB and **Q_social** at MSC = MSB. State whether the good is over- or under-produced.

items.label: 8.1 Government policies to correct market failure

steps.caption: MPC private cost; MSC = MPC + external cost.
MPB private benefit; MSB = MPB + external benefit.
Overproduction when negative externality — MSC > MSB at market Q.
Pigouvian tax/subsidy moves Q toward social optimum.
steps.embedHint: Tax per unit = external cost at optimum.
